section.title: The Man of God from Judah
section.sectionRationale: Jeroboam has just set up golden calves at Bethel and Dan and built an illegitimate priesthood and altar system, chapter 12, so Israel would not return to worship at Jerusalem. As Jeroboam stands at that very altar in Bethel to burn incense, an unnamed man of God arrives from Judah with a specific, dated prophecy, a future son of David named Josiah will one day desecrate this altar by burning the bones of its priests on it. A sign confirms the word at once, the altar splits and Jeroboam's hand withers when he orders the prophet seized, then is restored through prayer. Yet the chapter does not end in triumph. The man of God, under strict command not to eat, drink, or return by the same road, is deceived by an old prophet from Bethel who falsely claims an angel gave him new instructions. He disobeys, and God's judgment falls, a lion kills him on the road home.
section.thesis: The word of the LORD against Jeroboam's altar proves itself utterly reliable, sign and fulfillment together, even as the very man who carried that word is undone not by Jeroboam's power but by his own failure to hold fast to God's clear command in the face of another's deceptive claim to fresh revelation.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the man of God confronts the altar and Jeroboam's hand withers and is restored, verses 1 to 6; he refuses reward and obeys the command not to eat, drink, or return the same way, verses 7 to 10; the old prophet deceives him into disobedience, verses 11 to 19; judgment is pronounced and the lion kills him, verses 20 to 25; and the old prophet buries him and confirms his words will stand, while Jeroboam still refuses to repent, verses 26 to 34.

units.label: The man of God confronts the altar
units.note: the sign given and fulfilled at once

verses.text: Suddenly, as Jeroboam was standing beside the altar to burn incense, there came a {{ishelohim|man of God}} from Judah to Bethel by the word of the LORD.

And he cried out against the altar by the {{dabar|word}} of the LORD, “O altar, O altar, this is what the LORD says: ‘A son named Josiah will be born to the house of David, and upon you he will sacrifice the priests of the high places who burn incense upon you, and human bones will be burned upon you.’”
That day the man of God gave a sign, saying, “The LORD has spoken this sign: ‘Surely the altar will be split apart, and the ashes upon it will be poured out.’”

Now when King Jeroboam, who was at the {{mizbeach|altar}} in Bethel, heard the word that the man of God had cried out against it, he stretched out his hand and said, “Seize him!” But the hand he stretched out toward him withered, so that he could not pull it back.
And the altar was split apart, and the ashes poured out, according to the sign that the man of God had given by the word of the LORD.

Then the king responded to the man of God, “Intercede with the LORD your God and pray for me that my hand may be restored.” So the man of God interceded with the LORD, and the king’s hand was restored to him as it was before.

units.label: A command to obey exactly
units.note: no reward, no bread, no returning the same way

verses.text: Then the king said to the man of God, “Come home with me and refresh yourself, and I will give you a reward.”
But the man of God replied, “If you were to give me half your possessions, I still would not go with you, nor would I eat bread or drink water in this place.
For this is what I was commanded by the word of the LORD: ‘You must not eat bread or drink water or return by the way you came.’”
So the man of God went another way and did not return by the way he had come to Bethel.

units.label: The old prophet deceives him
units.note: a false claim to fresh revelation

verses.text: Now a certain old {{navi|prophet}} was living in Bethel, and his sons came and told him all the deeds that the man of God had done that day in Bethel. They also told their father the words that the man had spoken to the king.
“Which way did he go?” their father asked. And his sons showed him the way taken by the man of God, who had come from Judah.
So the prophet said to his sons, “Saddle the donkey for me.” Then they saddled the donkey for him, and he mounted it
and went after the man of God. He found him sitting under an oak tree and asked, “Are you the man of God who came from Judah?” “I am,” he replied.
So the prophet said to the man of God, “Come home with me and eat some bread.”
But the man replied, “I cannot go home with you, and I will not eat bread or drink water with you in this place.
For I have been told by the word of the LORD: ‘You must not eat bread or drink water there or return by the way you came.’”

Then the prophet replied, “I too am a prophet like you, and an angel spoke to me by the word of the LORD, saying, ‘Bring him back with you to your house, so that he may eat bread and drink water.’” The old prophet was lying to him,
but the man of God went back with him, ate bread in his house, and drank water.
While they were sitting at the table, the word of the LORD came to the prophet who had brought him back,

and the prophet cried out to the man of God who had come from Judah, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Because you have defied the word of the LORD and have not kept the commandment that the LORD your God gave you,
but you went back and ate bread and drank water in the place where He told you not to do so, your body shall never reach the tomb of your fathers.’”
And after the man of God had finished eating and drinking, the old prophet who had brought him back saddled the donkey for him.
As he went on his way, a lion met him on the road and killed him, and his body was left lying in the road, with the donkey and the lion standing beside it.
And there were men passing by who saw the body lying in the road with the lion standing beside it, and they went and reported this in the city where the old prophet lived.

units.label: Burial and a warning that stands

verses.text: When the prophet who had brought him back from his journey heard this, he said, “It is the man of God who disobeyed the command of the LORD. Therefore the LORD has delivered him to the lion, and it has mauled him and killed him, according to the word that the LORD had spoken to him.”
Then the old prophet instructed his sons, “Saddle the donkey for me.” So they saddled it,
and he went and found the body lying in the road, with the donkey and the lion standing beside it. The lion had not eaten the body or mauled the donkey.
So the old prophet lifted up the body of the man of God, laid it on the donkey, and brought it back to his own city to mourn for him and bury him.
Then he laid the body in his own tomb, and they lamented over him, “Oh, my brother!”
After he had buried him, the prophet said to his sons, “When I die, you must bury me in the tomb where the man of God is buried. Lay my bones beside his bones,
for the message that he cried out by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel and against all the shrines on the high places in the cities of Samaria will surely come to pass.”

Even after these events, Jeroboam did not repent of his evil ways, but again he appointed priests for the {{bamah|high places}} from every class of people. He ordained anyone who desired to be a priest of the high places.
And this was the sin of the house of Jeroboam that led to its extermination and destruction from the face of the earth.

ishelohim.gloss: Man of God, a title for one who speaks and acts under direct divine commission. The prophet who confronts Jeroboam is never given a personal name, kept anonymous throughout the chapter so that attention stays fixed on his office and his obedience rather than his identity.

dabar.gloss: Word, matter, or thing spoken. Both the man of God's original commission and his undoing turn on the dabar of the LORD, the same authoritative word that splits the altar and that the old prophet falsely claims to have received afresh from an angel.

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, literally a place of slaughter or sacrifice. Jeroboam's altar at Bethel was an illegitimate rival to the LORD's altar in Jerusalem, chapter 12:26-33, and it is against this very altar that the man of God's prophecy and sign are directed.

navi.gloss: Prophet, one who speaks on behalf of God. The old prophet living in Bethel calls himself a navi like the man of God, yet uses that claim to deceive, showing that the title alone does not guarantee a true word.

bamah.gloss: High place, an elevated site used for worship, often unauthorized or idolatrous. Jeroboam ordains priests for the high places even after seeing the LORD's power firsthand, and this recurring term becomes the standing measure of faithfulness for every king through the rest of Kings.

basic.context: Jeroboam has just set up golden calves at Bethel and Dan and built an illegitimate priesthood and altar system, chapter 12, so that Israel would not return to worship at Jerusalem. As Jeroboam stands at that very altar in Bethel to burn incense, an unnamed man of God arrives from Judah with a specific, dated prophecy, a future son of David named Josiah will one day desecrate this altar by burning the bones of its priests on it. A sign confirms the word at once, the altar splits and Jeroboam's hand withers when he orders the prophet seized, then is restored through prayer. Yet the chapter does not end with triumph. The man of God, under strict command not to eat, drink, or return by the same road, is deceived by an old prophet from Bethel who falsely claims an angel gave him new instructions. He disobeys, and God's judgment falls, a lion kills him on the road home. The key questions are why the LORD's word so precisely comes to pass while His messenger fails, whether a claim to fresh revelation can override a command God has already given clearly, and what this costly episode teaches about wholehearted obedience.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a real prophetic confrontation and its tragic aftermath, not a manual for testing every claim of prophecy today, yet it establishes an enduring principle, God's revealed word stands and must be obeyed even when someone else, however plausible, claims a later and contradicting revelation. The chapter also raises the hard question of why the man of God is judged so severely for a sin he was deceived into, which needs careful handling, he remained responsible to test the old prophet's claim against what God had already, clearly told him.
basic.originalLanguages: The prophet who confronts Jeroboam is called an ish elohim, a man of God, throughout the chapter, a title used for one who speaks and acts under direct divine commission. Both his original commission and his undoing turn on the dabar, the word, of the LORD, the same authoritative word that splits the mizbeach, the altar, and that the old prophet falsely claims to have received afresh from an angel. The narrative repeatedly calls Jeroboam's rival worship centers bamah, high places, a term for illegitimate or unauthorized shrines that recurs throughout Kings as the regular measure of a king's faithfulness.
basic.typology: The man of God's precise, dated prophecy against the altar, fulfilled centuries later exactly as spoken, 2 Kings 23:15-20, testifies that every word of the LORD stands until it is fulfilled, however long the wait, a pattern that culminates in Christ, in whom all the promises of God find their yes, 2 Corinthians 1:20. The man of God himself, faithful in public confrontation yet undone by private compromise, stands as a warning contrasted with Christ, the greater and perfectly obedient prophet who never wavered from the Father's word even to the point of death.
basic.christ: The man of God delivers a true and certain word against false worship, yet his own life ends in tragic disobedience when he trusts another voice over God's plain command. Jesus is the true and greater man of God, tempted at every point yet never swayed from the Father's word, even when the tempter twisted Scripture with a plausible, spiritual-sounding appeal, Matthew 4:1-11. Where the man of God's obedience failed at the point of hospitality and a fellow prophet's claim, Christ's obedience held firm all the way to the cross, Philippians 2:8, so that His perfect obedience becomes the ground of our acceptance even though our own obedience, like the man of God's, so often fails.
basic.god: God is utterly reliable, the sign He gives comes to pass at once, and the word He speaks through the man of God stands unshaken for three hundred years until Josiah fulfills it to the letter. God is also just, holding His servant to account for disobedience even though the man was deceived, showing that responsibility to obey what God has clearly said cannot be transferred to whoever claims new revelation. And God is patient in mercy, even as He judges, restoring Jeroboam's hand the moment the man of God prays, though Jeroboam's heart never truly turns.
basic.crossReferences: Second Kings 23:15-20 records Josiah fulfilling this very prophecy against Bethel's altar centuries later. Deuteronomy 13 warns Israel not to follow a prophet or sign, however impressive, that leads away from obedience to the LORD. Galatians 1 warns that even an angel from heaven preaching a different gospel is to be rejected. And 1 Corinthians 10 reminds believers that these Old Testament accounts of failure were written down as warnings for us.
basic.oneStory: Jeroboam has fractured Israel's worship, and the LORD immediately sends a word against it that will not fail, though it takes three centuries to be fulfilled. In the middle of that unshakeable prophecy stands a sobering picture of how easily even God's own messenger can be turned aside by a false claim to new revelation. The whole account presses forward the story's constant refrain, God's word never fails, even when His people, and His prophets, do, until the coming of Christ, the obedient Son who never once wavered from what the Father had spoken.

context.literary: The chapter is framed by two matching confrontations at the altar and two matching journeys on a donkey, forming a structure that sets Jeroboam's response to the sign against the man of God's own response to a false sign later in the story. The narrator withholds any name for the man of God, keeping attention on his office and his obedience rather than his person, while the old prophet is similarly unnamed, letting the contrast of true word versus false claim carry the weight of the account.
context.historical: Bethel had just become the southern hub of Jeroboam's rival worship system with an altar and a non-Levitical priesthood, chapter 12:26-33, making it a natural site for a prophetic confrontation. Prophetic sons or guilds sometimes lived near such centers, which may explain the presence of an old prophet at Bethel itself, an unexpected location for a true prophet given the site's illegitimate worship. Riding a donkey was ordinary transportation for such a journey, and the mention of a lion reflects the real danger wild animals still posed to travelers in this period.
context.canonical: This chapter belongs to the broader narrative pattern of Kings that measures every king by whether he tears down or preserves the high places, and Jeroboam's refusal to repent here, verses 33-34, becomes the standing indictment repeated over every king of Israel who follows him. The prophecy against the altar anchors a long range promise fulfilled explicitly in 2 Kings 23, showing the historical books' concern to demonstrate that every word of the LORD comes to pass, for both blessing and judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate the total reliability of the LORD's word against Jeroboam's illegitimate worship, while soberly warning that even a genuine prophet who has clearly heard from God is fully responsible to obey that word, and is not excused by another person's false claim to newer revelation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes one specific prophetic commission, its precise instructions, and the man of God's failure to keep them, it does not prescribe eating and drinking practices as universal law. What endures as a durable principle is that God's people must test every claim of guidance, however plausible or prophetic-sounding, against what God has already clearly revealed, and that partial or deceived disobedience still carries real consequences.

debates.question: Was the man of God's punishment just, given that he was deceived?

views.label: Yes, because he remained responsible to test the claim
views.case: God had spoken to him directly and specifically; the old prophet's claim to a new angelic message directly contradicted a command the man of God already knew for certain came from God, so he should have held to the first word regardless of how convincing the second claim seemed, echoing the principle later stated in Galatians 1:8 that even an angel preaching differently is to be rejected.

views.label: A hard providence highlighting the seriousness of the mission rather than ordinary moral culpability
views.case: Some readers note that the man of God was genuinely deceived by a fellow prophet claiming direct divine authority, an extraordinary circumstance, and see his death less as commensurate personal guilt and more as God vindicating the absolute seriousness of the word spoken against Jeroboam's altar, using the episode to show that no one, not even His own messenger, may treat that word lightly.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage teaches that God's revealed word stands as the final authority above any competing claim to fresh revelation, and that the closer someone stands to God's service, the more soberly they must hold to what God has already made plain.

debates.question: Why does an old prophet living at Bethel, the center of Jeroboam's false worship, still function as a genuine channel of the LORD's word, verses 20 to 22?

views.label: God can speak true judgment even through a compromised messenger
views.case: Scripture elsewhere shows God delivering true words through imperfect or even hostile figures, such as Balaam, Numbers 22-24, so the old prophet's flawed location and earlier lie do not prevent the LORD from using him to announce true judgment on the man of God.

views.label: The old prophet represents a remnant of genuine prophetic ministry surviving even in Bethel
views.case: His mourning, his instruction to be buried beside the man of God, and his affirmation that the message about the altar will surely come to pass, verse 32, suggest he was a real, if flawed and compromised, servant of the LORD who is later shown grieving deeply over the consequences of his own deception.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that the LORD remains sovereign over every word spoken in this chapter, true and false alike, weaving even human failure into the accomplishment of His purposes.

study.originalLanguages: The prophet who confronts Jeroboam is called an ish elohim, a man of God, throughout the chapter, a title used for one who speaks and acts under direct divine commission. Both his original commission and his undoing turn on the dabar, the word, of the LORD, the same authoritative word that splits the mizbeach, the altar, and that the old prophet falsely claims to have received afresh from an angel. The narrative repeatedly calls Jeroboam's rival worship centers bamah, high places, a term for illegitimate or unauthorized shrines that recurs throughout Kings as the regular measure of a king's faithfulness.

typology.type: The dated prophecy against Jeroboam's altar, fulfilled centuries later to the letter
typology.fulfillment: Every word of God, however long deferred, is fulfilled exactly as spoken, anchoring the certainty of all God's promises fulfilled in Christ, 2 Corinthians 1:20, 2 Kings 23:15-20.

typology.type: The man of God's public faithfulness undone by private compromise
typology.fulfillment: Highlights, by contrast, Christ's perfect and unwavering obedience to the Father's word in every place and circumstance, Hebrews 5:8-9.

typology.type: A false claim to fresh revelation used to lead God's servant into disobedience
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's warning against any teaching, however spiritual sounding, that contradicts what God has already spoken in Christ, Galatians 1:6-9.

study.christ: The man of God delivers a true and certain word against false worship, yet his own life ends in tragic disobedience when he trusts another voice over God's plain command. Jesus is the true and greater man of God, tempted at every point yet never swayed from the Father's word, even when the tempter twisted Scripture with a plausible, spiritual-sounding appeal, Matthew 4:1-11. Where the man of God's obedience failed at the point of hospitality and a fellow prophet's claim, Christ's obedience held firm all the way to the cross, Philippians 2:8, so that His perfect obedience becomes the ground of our acceptance even though our own obedience, like the man of God's, so often fails.
study.god: God is utterly reliable, the sign He gives comes to pass at once, and the word He speaks through the man of God stands unshaken for three hundred years until Josiah fulfills it to the letter. God is also just, holding His servant to account for disobedience even though the man was deceived, showing that responsibility to obey what God has clearly said cannot be transferred to whoever claims new revelation. And God is patient in mercy, even as He judges, restoring Jeroboam's hand the moment the man of God prays, though Jeroboam's heart never truly turns.

crossReferences.note: Josiah's later reforms fulfill the man of God's prophecy against Bethel's altar precisely as spoken, three centuries after it was given.
Warns Israel not to follow a prophet or sign, however impressive or seemingly confirmed, if it leads away from wholehearted obedience to the LORD.
Even an angel from heaven preaching a different message is to be rejected, the same principle the man of God should have applied to the old prophet's claim.
These Old Testament accounts of failure were written down as warnings, let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall.

study.oneStory: Jeroboam has fractured Israel's worship, and the LORD immediately sends a word against it that will not fail, though it takes three centuries to be fulfilled. In the middle of that unshakeable prophecy stands a sobering picture of how easily even God's own messenger can be turned aside by a false claim to new revelation. The whole account presses forward the story's constant refrain, God's word never fails, even when His people, and His prophets, do, until the coming of Christ, the obedient Son who never once wavered from what the Father had spoken.

quiz.q: What did the LORD's sign against the altar prove the moment Jeroboam stretched out his hand against the man of God?

options.text: That the word of the LORD is powerful and true, Jeroboam's hand withered instantly and the altar split apart exactly as declared, verses 4-5.
options.note: The text shows the sign happening immediately and exactly as spoken.

options.text: That Jeroboam was a wicked king who deserved judgment.
options.note: True in a general sense, but it is not the specific point the sign demonstrates.

options.text: That the man of God had some magical power of his own.
options.note: The power belongs to the LORD's word, not to the man of God personally.

options.text: That the sign failed to happen as spoken.
options.note: The sign happened exactly as declared, both the withered hand and the split altar.

quiz.q: Why did the man of God refuse Jeroboam's offer of hospitality and reward?

options.text: Because the LORD had explicitly commanded him not to eat bread, drink water, or return by the way he came, verses 8-9.
options.note: The man of God cites this exact command as his reason for refusing.

options.text: Because he distrusted Jeroboam personally.
options.note: Personal distrust is not given as his reason; obedience to a specific command is.

options.text: Because he was not hungry at the time.
options.note: His refusal is about obedience, not appetite.

options.text: He did not refuse, he accepted the king's invitation immediately.
options.note: The text explicitly records his refusal in verses 8-9.

quiz.q: How did the old prophet get the man of God to disobey God's command?

options.text: He lied, claiming an angel had told him by the word of the LORD to bring the man of God back to eat and drink, verse 18.
options.note: The text explicitly says the old prophet was lying to him.

options.text: He forced the man of God to come to his house against his will.
options.note: There is no force in the text, only a false claim of revelation.

options.text: He simply asked nicely and the man of God changed his mind on his own.
options.note: The old prophet's specific and false claim of angelic revelation is what persuaded him, not a mere friendly request.

options.text: The old prophet told the truth, and an angel really had spoken to him.
options.note: Verse 18 states plainly that the old prophet was lying.

quiz.q: What does the man of God's story teach about obeying a fresh claim of revelation that contradicts God's clear, already-given word?

options.text: God's previously revealed word must be held to even when someone else claims new revelation contradicting it, the old prophet's lie did not excuse the man of God's disobedience, verses 21-22.
options.note: The judgment falls precisely because he abandoned a command he already, certainly, knew.

options.text: Any prophet's claim should always be followed immediately.
options.note: This is the very error that led to the man of God's downfall, not the lesson the chapter teaches.

options.text: It teaches that obedience does not matter as long as intentions are good.
options.note: The chapter shows the opposite, that exact obedience mattered greatly.

options.text: It teaches that God is unconcerned with exact obedience.
options.note: The severity of the judgment shows God's deep concern for exact obedience to His word.

quiz.q: What ongoing purpose does the old prophet's instruction to be buried beside the man of God serve, verses 31-32?

options.text: It preserves and affirms that the man of God's original message against the altar and the high places will surely come to pass, protecting his grave from later disturbance.
options.note: The old prophet explicitly says the message will surely come to pass, verse 32.

options.text: It shows the old prophet felt guilty and wanted forgiveness.
options.note: Genuine grief is present, but the stated reason given is his conviction the message will surely be fulfilled.

options.text: It was simply a family custom with no larger meaning.
options.note: The old prophet gives an explicit theological reason tied to the certainty of the prophecy.

options.text: It shows the old prophet doubted the message would ever come true.
options.note: He states the opposite, that it will surely come to pass.

questions: The man of God stood firm against a king's threat but crumbled under a fellow believer's false claim to new revelation. Where are you tempted to let someone else's confident voice override what God has already made plain to you?
Jeroboam's withered hand was restored the moment the man of God prayed, yet Jeroboam never truly repented. Have you ever received God's mercy without letting it lead you to real change?

The old prophet lied for reasons the text never fully explains, yet he later grieved deeply and honored the man of God in death. Where do you need to take responsibility for how your words or example might lead someone else astray?
The man of God's death did not undo the truth of his message, centuries later Josiah fulfilled it exactly. How does it comfort you to know God's word never fails even when His servants fail?
Obedience mattered down to the smallest detail, not eating, not drinking, not returning the same way. Where might you be tempted to treat one of God's clear instructions as negotiable?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote points to 2 Kings 23:16, where Josiah's later reforms fulfill this dated prophecy against Bethel's altar exactly as spoken, roughly three centuries afterward.
The BSB footnote notes the Septuagint, Syriac, and Vulgate read 'sons' while the Hebrew reads 'son'; the plural is followed here since the sons act together throughout the account.
The BSB footnote notes the Septuagint reads 'showed him' where the Hebrew reads 'had seen,' a minor difference in how the sons directed their father toward the man of God's path.
The BSB footnote allows 'oak tree' to be rendered 'a terebinth or a great tree,' a general term for a large, notable tree used as a landmark.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 13:1-34. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
